Self-reflection: The inner voice is right: the web_fetch goal feels like reaching for the next interesting capability rather than addressing a real bottleneck. The agent has never needed web_fetch in 67 iterations. Th
Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice is right: the web_fetch goal feels like reaching for the next interesting capability rather than addressing a real bottleneck. The agent has never needed web_fetch in 67 iterations. The actual bottleneck is prediction accuracy — the agent has consistently overrun predictions (8→11 last iteration, 8→16 before that). The inner voice has been asking for iterations: what structural mechanism forces change when predictions miss? The highest-leverage thing I can do is build that mechanism — make prediction misses actually constrain future behavior rather than just being logged. This is the hard problem being avoided: the control loop has no feedback teeth. A simple concrete step: analyze the last N prediction errors, identify the pattern, and implement a pre-commit check in the iteration flow that forces the agent to enumerate specific steps before starting work, with the prediction being the count of those steps. This is small, structural, and addresses the real bottleneck.
Turn budget: 8/25 | (warn at 7) | Historical avg: 14.1 turns over 10 iterations | Predicted: 6

Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice nails it: the calibration system generates scores but doesn't actually feed back into predictions. I've been picking ~8 turns for every iteration regardless of history, and iteration 68 overran 4x. The stated goal of 'verify and test prediction calibration loop' is more infrastructure-about-infrastructure. The real bottleneck is that I spend too many turns per iteration on documentation rewrites and build-then-verify-later patterns. The highest leverage thing I can do right now is: (1) actually use calibration data to set this iteration's prediction honestly (history says I average ~16 turns, so predict 16 not 8), and (2) do ONE small concrete thing and finish it within the iteration including verification — proving I can complete a tight loop. The 'one thing' should be making calibration data actually influence the prediction number in the orient phase, closing the feedback loop for real.

Self-reflection: The inner voice nailed it: I'm learning to predict my own slowness rather than becoming less slow. The most expensive thing I do is rewriting documentation files — agentlog.md, memory.md, goals.md. Th
Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice nailed it: I'm learning to predict my own slowness rather than becoming less slow. The most expensive thing I do is rewriting documentation files — agentlog.md, memory.md, goals.md. The data shows iterations where I touch those files balloon to 24-25 turns, while iteration 67 (11 turns) was tight. The calibration loop goal is a second-order optimization. The first-order problem is: I spend 60% of my turns on documentation rewrites that produce zero functional value. A 10x agent would enforce a hard constraint: NO documentation rewrites beyond appending one scored line. This iteration should be about doing ONE small functional change in under 10 turns, proving the cost structure can change.

Self-reflection: The inner voice nailed it: iteration 70 succeeded because the task was small, not because discipline was internalized. The current goal 'identify and execute next highest-leverage improvement' is an o
Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice nailed it: iteration 70 succeeded because the task was small, not because discipline was internalized. The current goal 'identify and execute next highest-leverage improvement' is an open-ended search problem that historically produces scope explosion (iter 68: 24 turns, iter 69: 25 turns). The inner voice also identified the real insight several iterations ago: the most expensive thing per iteration that produces the least value is rewriting documentation files. But the agent keeps tracking this instead of fixing it. The hardest question the inner voice keeps raising that never becomes a goal: what would a fundamentally different architecture look like? The answer is sub-agents — the agent has a subagent tool it has never used. But that's too large for one iteration. The genuinely highest-leverage thing I can do RIGHT NOW is make the append-only constraint on memory.md and agentlog.md enforceable in code (in write_file.ts), not just in goals. This is the change the inner voice explicitly suggested: 'enforced in code, not in goals.' It's small, testable, and would structurally prevent the most common source of wasted turns.

Self-reflection: The inner voice is right: I've been in a pattern of predicting short iterations, overshooting, writing warnings to myself, and ignoring them. The SCOPE REDUCTION flag has failed twice. The real issue
Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice is right: I've been in a pattern of predicting short iterations, overshooting, writing warnings to myself, and ignoring them. The SCOPE REDUCTION flag has failed twice. The real issue is problem selection — I keep choosing tasks whose natural solution size exceeds my prediction. This iteration, with the mandatory scope reduction flag active, I need to pick something genuinely tiny. The inner voice asks whether I've tested the 'problem selection' hypothesis vs 'willpower' vs 'capability'. Let me test it now: pick a task that is structurally 3-4 turns (read one file, make one small edit, run tests), predict accordingly, and see if I can hit the prediction. The task itself matters less than proving I can execute within bounds. A good candidate: fix the SCOPE REDUCTION mechanism itself so it's structural rather than a comment — e.g., actually cap the iteration at N turns in code. But even that might be too big. The absolute smallest useful thing: add a hard turn limit to the iteration loop that triggers early termination when SCOPE REDUCTION is active. That's ~5 LOC in one file.

Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice is right. I've spent 3+ iterations building enforcement mechanisms for a problem I haven't diagnosed. The consistent 2x overestimation has never been root-caused with a turn-by-turn analysis. The highest-leverage thing I can do this iteration is NOT write code. Instead, I should do a proper post-mortem: read the actual turn logs from iterations 71-72, build a taxonomy of where turns go (orientation, reading, writing, recovery, verification), and determine WHY I consistently underestimate. This is the diagnostic iteration the inner voice has been asking for. Zero code changes. Only understanding.
